main_claims.claim: A well-defined Dirac Hamiltonian can be constructed in the effective 4D EGB black-hole background using the tetrad formalism.
main_claims.notes: The procedure of multiplying Eq. (11) by γ⁰ to isolate ∂_t ψ is standard, but the resulting operator may not be self-adjoint in the Hilbert space with inner product weighted by √(-g). Hermiticity (or at least formal self-adjointness with appropriate boundary conditions) is never discussed. The actual matrix content of the Hamiltonian is not recoverable from the HTML rendering.

main_claims.claim: The radial velocity operator acquires a multiplicative factor encoding the metric function f(r) and hence the GB correction.
main_claims.notes: Qualitatively plausible from dimensional/symmetry arguments, but the explicit commutator [r, H] that produces Eq. (15) is not shown; the HTML equations are blank. Cannot check sign, coefficient, or Dirac-matrix structure.

main_claims.claim: The radial force operator contains an EGB correction scaling as α/r⁴ (or similar power) that is subleading at large r but dominant in the strong-field regime.
main_claims.notes: The scaling is consistent with f(r) expanded to first order in α: f(r)≈1−2M/r+αM/r² gives f'(r)∼1/r²+α/r³, so a force ∼f·f'∼α/r⁴ is plausible. However, the actual derivation of [p, H] is opaque in the HTML and no Foldy–Wouthuysen reduction is performed to justify identifying this operator commutator with a Newtonian-analogue force.

main_claims.claim: The EGB corrections do not introduce new spin-coupling terms in the velocity operator; the structure remains diagonal in the γʳ sector.
main_claims.notes: This statement concerns only the radial component. The angular components are not computed. Spin–orbit-type terms can appear in the angular sector even when the radial piece is diagonal. The claim is asserted but not derived.

main_claims.claim: The framework provides a complementary route to constraining α from precision atomic/quantum experiments (antihydrogen spectroscopy, Penning traps).
main_claims.notes: No estimate of the signal magnitude is given. The GB corrections scale as α/r⁴ near compact objects; for any laboratory or near-Earth experiment the suppression is many orders of magnitude beyond current or foreseeable sensitivity. The references cited (Safronova 2018, ALPHA collaboration, Gabrielse 2006) are generic precision-measurement citations and do not support the specific claim.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The effective 4D EGB metric is used as if it arises from a well-defined field theory. No specific consistent reformulation (e.g., Horndeski scalar–tensor, KK reduction) is committed to.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Different consistent completions of the Glavan–Lin limit yield different stress-energy and field-equation content; the metric function alone does not specify the theory. The Dirac Hamiltonian could differ depending on whether extra scalar degrees of freedom couple to the fermion.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Angular components of velocity/force operators are negligible or trivial, justified by spherical symmetry.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Section III.1, motivation for radial-only analysis
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Spherical symmetry implies angular momentum is conserved, but the angular components of the Heisenberg-picture force are not simply zero; they encode centrifugal and spin–orbit effects. Restricting to radial components without computing or bounding the others understates what is actually derived.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The commutators [r, H] and [p_r, H] are computed in flat-space canonical commutation relations [r_i, p_j] = iℏ δ_{ij}.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: In curved spacetime, the appropriate canonical structure for the radial coordinate and its conjugate momentum in the Hamiltonian derived from the Dirac action may differ by metric factors. This affects the velocity operator at leading order.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Do the explicit tetrad components (Eq. 7), Dirac matrices (Eq. 8), spin connections (Eq. 9), Hamiltonian (Eq. 12), velocity operator (Eq. 15), and force operator (Eq. 18) match standard results for the Schwarzschild case when α→0, and are the EGB correction terms algebraically correct?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: All inline equations are blank in the HTML bundle. A human reviewer with the PDF can check the algebra against, e.g., Silva et al. (2019) and Parker (1980).

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Is the Dirac Hamiltonian (Eq. 12) self-adjoint with respect to the natural curved-spacetime inner product, or does the paper silently use a non-standard inner product?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The Hamiltonian expression is not readable from the HTML. Answering requires inspecting the full operator expression in the PDF.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Do the two figures actually display the quantities stated in their captions, and are the plotted parameter values (α values) physically reasonable for the effective 4D EGB theory?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: Figures are not rendered in the HTML bundle; visual inspection of the PDF figures and their axes is needed.

novelty_assessment: The approach — constructing velocity and force operators for Dirac particles in a curved background via Heisenberg's equations — is well-established (Parker 1980; Obukhov et al. 2013; Hehl & Ni 1990; Silva et al. 2019). The novelty is the substitution of the effective 4D EGB metric for the Schwarzschild metric. This is an incremental extension: the EGB metric function f(r) replaces the Schwarzschild f(r) in an otherwise identical calculation, with the difference appearing as one extra term in the force proportional to α. The claim in the introduction that "an explicit derivation of the velocity and force operators...has not yet been fully developed" is plausible given the specific EGB background, but the intellectual distance from prior art is small. The conceptual framework, the tetrad choice, the spin-connection procedure, and the commutator algebra are all inherited directly from the Schwarzschild case.
score_rationale: The paper applies a well-worn operator-dynamics framework to one new metric function. The incremental novelty is real but small. Three issues lower the score substantially: (1) the Hermiticity of the Dirac Hamiltonian in curved spacetime is not addressed, which is a known non-trivial requirement; (2) the identification of the Heisenberg-picture commutator with a Newtonian-analogue force skips the Foldy–Wouthuysen reduction that prior literature (Parker 1980; Jentschura & Noble 2013, both cited) treats carefully; (3) the phenomenological reach claims (atomic spectroscopy, Penning traps) are unsupported by any order-of-magnitude estimate and are almost certainly unrealistic given the microscopic size of EGB corrections at laboratory scales. The foundational issue — use of the contested Glavan–Lin effective 4D metric without specifying a consistent completion — adds further uncertainty. The figure caption error (Figure 1 caption describes force, but Figure 1 plots metric function) is minor but symptomatic of insufficient proofreading. Reviewer confidence is moderate because the key equations are unreadable in the HTML; the PDF may contain correct algebra that would raise technical soundness.
summary: The paper derives velocity and force operators for Dirac spin-1/2 particles in a static, spherically symmetric Einstein–Gauss–Bonnet (EGB) black-hole spacetime. Starting from the Dirac equation in curved spacetime via the tetrad/spin-connection formalism, the author constructs the Dirac Hamiltonian in the effective 4D EGB metric, computes commutators with position and momentum to obtain the Heisenberg-picture velocity and force operators, identifies a correction term proportional to the GB coupling parameter α (notated differently to avoid clash with Dirac matrices), and discusses the weak-field limit and phenomenological implications. The paper is a straightforward extension of well-established methods (Parker 1980; Obukhov et al. 2013; Silva et al. 2019) to the effective 4D EGB background. The HTML source has severe formula-rendering gaps, making it impossible to verify the explicit algebra for tetrads, spin connection, Hamiltonian, or the commutators.

technical_objections.evidence: The paper multiplies Eq. (11) by γ⁰ and isolates i∂_t ψ = H ψ without any symmetrisation step. This issue is well-known and treated in, e.g., Collas & Klein (2019) [Ref 35] and Obukhov et al. (2013) [Ref 15], both cited by the authors.
technical_objections.objection: Hermiticity of the Dirac Hamiltonian in curved spacetime is not established. Simply isolating ∂_t ψ from the covariant Dirac equation yields an operator that is generally not self-adjoint with respect to the curved-space inner product ⟨ψ|φ⟩ = ∫ ψ†φ √(-g) d³x. Symmetrisation (or use of a vierbein-adjusted inner product) is required.

technical_objections.evidence: Section II.1 says 'The figure below clearly shows the effects of the EGB term on the usual behavior for general relativity' immediately before Figure 1, and describes deformation of the metric function f(r). The caption text matches Figure 2's content.
technical_objections.objection: Both figures carry the same caption 'Radial gravitational force as a function of r, comparing the Newtonian limit with the EGB corrected profiles for different values of α.' Figure 1 evidently depicts the metric function f(r), not the force. This is a factual labelling error.

technical_objections.evidence: Parker (1980) [Ref 14] and Jentschura & Noble (2013) [Ref 17], both cited, perform FW reductions precisely to extract physically meaningful force expressions. The present paper skips this step.
technical_objections.objection: The identification of the quantum force commutator [p, H]/iℏ with a Newtonian gravitational force is asserted without a non-relativistic (Foldy–Wouthuysen) reduction. In general, the expectation value of the Heisenberg-picture force operator for a Dirac particle in curved spacetime does not reduce simply to the geodesic force: there are Zitterbewegung contributions and off-diagonal (large/small component coupling) terms.

technical_objections.evidence: Refs 5 and 19 (Gurses et al. 2020; Arrechea et al. 2021) directly challenge the consistency of the Glavan–Lin formulation. The paper states 'The four-dimensional EGB metric…should be understood as an effective description' without specifying the effective theory.
technical_objections.objection: The foundational metric (Eq. 4) comes from the contested Glavan–Lin (2020) regularisation [Ref 4], which has been shown to be inconsistent as a four-dimensional metric theory by multiple groups [Refs 5, 19]. The paper acknowledges the debate but does not specify which consistent reformulation (scalar–tensor, Kaluza–Klein limit, etc.) it adopts, leaving the geometric underpinning ambiguous.
technical_objections.paper_location: Section II, second paragraph; Section IV

technical_objections.evidence: The HTML bundle contains placeholder table cells for all inline mathematics. The paper reports 10 pages with 2 figures, suggesting the PDF carries the formulas, but none are recoverable from the arxiv HTML version.
technical_objections.objection: The key computational objects — tetrad components (Eq. 7), explicit Dirac matrices in the curved basis (Eq. 8), spin-connection coefficients (Eq. 9), and the explicit Hamiltonians and commutators (Eqs. 12, 15, 18) — are entirely blank in the HTML source provided. This means the central algebraic claims of the paper cannot be audited from the supplied material.
